# Preface

## I. The signal

Over two million people are watching a single lecturer explain civilization, power, and the future. The Predictive History channel is growing faster than any comparable platform in the long-form history and geopolitics space — faster than CaspianReport, Fall of Civilizations, or Zeihan — adding roughly two thousand subscribers a day. By March 2026, the channel's trajectory had drawn the attention of mainstream media; a Tucker Carlson interview that month drew over two million views in its first week, marking the point at which Jiang crossed from YouTube niche to broader public notice. The ideas found their audience first; mainstream media followed.

That growth is not an accident. It is evidence that the questions Jiang is asking — about how societies form, how legitimacy is imagined, and how the past can be used to read the future — resonate with a public that existing institutions have left underserved. The gap between Predictive History's two million subscribers and the Cambridge Centre for Geopolitics's ten thousand says something about what audiences want and what universities provide. Two million people did not subscribe because of production quality — the channel is a man with a whiteboard. They subscribed because the explanations land. The same public that has access to every university lecture, every think tank paper, every foreign policy podcast chose this. That is not a YouTube stat. It is a referendum on the failure of existing institutions to explain the world in a way that satisfies people who are actually paying attention.

The predictions are part of the signal. Jiang predicted the Iran war, Trump's reelection, and the economic forces driving both — years in advance, on camera. When a thinker's predictions prove accurate, the honest response is not worship; it is scrutiny. What framework produced those calls? Is it a system or a streak?

And there is the question of how he teaches. Jiang is delivering civilizational philosophy, game theory, and geopolitical analysis in a lecture format — and millions follow it. The lectures work not because the material is simplified but because the pedagogy is unusual: narrative-driven, cumulative, built on tension and participation rather than syllabus compliance. A lecturer who can make an audience argue about Kant, the Nephilim, and the American meritocracy in the same series is doing something that most universities cannot.

## II. The system

No one has stated Jiang's philosophy as a system, in plain language, and then tested it against reality. This book does that.

The central claim is not that every detail is beyond dispute. It is that Jiang's thought is best read as a **civilizational theory of formation, legitimacy, and strategic action** — not as a loose collection of takes on the news. Across five lecture series — Geo-Strategy, Civilization, Secret History, Game Theory, and Great Books — he builds a cumulative argument about how societies form themselves, how power is imagined and maintained, and how the past can be used to read the future. But YouTube lectures are fragments. Ideas arrive in fifty-minute sessions, absorbed once, rarely revisited in sequence. The cumulative argument is easy to miss when the medium is a playlist.

**Predictive History** is a multivolume work — one volume for each lecture series. This book is **Volume 1**, grounded in the **Geo-Strategy** series; later volumes will take up the sibling series on their own terms. The book states the theory, follows its arc, and then asks how well it holds up. It also pays attention to the pedagogical method as well as the intellectual content — what Jiang's teaching reveals about how complex ideas can be made accessible without being flattened.

If Jiang is even partially right — that we are inside a civilizational transition, that the mechanisms of legitimacy and formation are shifting, that the institutions built to interpret the world are themselves products of the order that is ending — then the stakes of understanding his framework are not academic. They are navigational. The question is not whether you agree with every claim. The question is whether you can afford to ignore a system of thought that has, so far, read the board more accurately than the people paid to read it.

## III. The test

This volume uses **one chapter for each Geo-Strategy lecture** — twelve chapters for twelve class sessions — so the book preserves the **arc of the teaching**, not the shape of a generic textbook. Exposition and analysis interleave as they did in the room. Each Part I chapter ends with **at least three predictions**, clearly stated and keyed to the project's prediction registry, so the book's forward-looking claims are visible before any scoring.

**Part II** (after Chapter 12) uses deep web and news research to test those predictions against the record — triangulating sources where it matters — and ends with an overall evaluation of the series's forecasts, without pretending philosophy collapses into a spreadsheet. Throughout, the argument stays tied to **transcript-backed material** from the curated lecture corpus, not to secondhand summaries.

This is not an official statement on anyone's behalf. It is not a substitute for listening to the lectures themselves if you want the voice, timing, and rhetorical context in full. And it is not a promise that philosophy can be settled like an accounting ledger: the appendices on predictions and divergence are tools for clarity, not trophies.

The book is for anyone who wants **clarity first**: a careful map of a distinctive philosophy, then an honest look at how well that map travels. Anyone who cares about civilizational thought as a living practice, not a museum piece. And anyone who, watching the signal grow, wants to understand what is actually being said — and whether it holds.

For the thesis threads, chapter order, and suggested reading paths, see **Introduction**.
